name: Sphinx
abilities: Petrifying Gaze: Anyone who looks directly at her face turns instantly to stone
Serpent Hair: Living, venomous snakes that writhe and strike independently
Flight: Golden wings enable swift aerial movement
description:
Explore the legendary creatures and mythical beings of this tradition.

This section is currently under development. We are working to bring you comprehensive information about
                    Greek creatures.

Check back soon for detailed entries!

weaknesses: Mirrors: Cannot see her own reflection without petrifying herself
Indirect Vision: Can be fought by reflecting her gaze or using periphery
Mortal Body: Despite her powers, her physical form can be wounded
Emotional Vulnerability: Attachment can cloud her judgment
habitat: Solitary caves and temples, places of desolation
behavior: Victim turned protector, vengeful yet sympathetic, isolation-driven

christian.overview: The Sphinx as guardian asking riddles invites comparison with biblical cherubim and Wisdom personified at the gates.

biblicalParallels.description: The living creatures with human and lion faces echo the Sphinx's hybrid form.
Cherubim guarding Eden parallel the Sphinx's threshold guardian role.
Wisdom calling at the gates parallels the Sphinx's riddle.

patristicInterpretation.summary: Church Fathers saw Egyptian hybrid symbolism as containing fragments of truth obscured by idolatry.

sources.author: Clement of Alexandria
sources.interpretation: The Sphinx's riddle about human nature finds its answer in Christ.

medievalSymbolism.bestiaryMeaning: Union of strength (lion) with wisdom (human) properly fulfilled in Christ.
medievalSymbolism.moralLesson: The riddle about human stages points to mortality and the need for faith.

christian.typologicalReading: The Sphinx's riddle finds ultimate answer in Christian anthropology: created in God's image, fallen, and restored in Christ.
scholarlyReferences: Assmann, Jan. Moses the Egyptian. Harvard University Press, 1997.

keyMyths.title: Oedipus and the Riddle
keyMyths.description: The most famous myth features Oedipus solving the Sphinx's riddle: "What walks on four legs in the morning, two in the afternoon, and three in the evening?" Oedipus correctly answered "man," leading to the Sphinx's demise.

keyMyths.title: The Sphinx's Arrival in Thebes
keyMyths.description: Hera, angered by the Thebans, sent the Sphinx to plague the city. The Sphinx perched outside Thebes and demanded that all who passed answer her riddle, devouring those who failed.

keyMyths.title: Laius's Curse and the Sphinx
keyMyths.description: The Sphinx's presence in Thebes is often linked to the curse placed upon Laius, Oedipus's father, for violating Xanthus. The Sphinx's torment of Thebes was a manifestation of this divine retribution.

keyMyths.title: The Sphinx's Suicide
keyMyths.description: Upon Oedipus correctly answering the riddle, the Sphinx, in despair and defeat, threw herself from her high rock, plummeting to her death. This ended her reign of terror over Thebes.

keyMyths.title: The Sphinx as Guardian
keyMyths.description: While the Sphinx is primarily known for the Oedipus myth, some accounts depict her as a guardian figure, protecting sacred places or knowledge. This protective role is less emphasized than her destructive one.

sources.name: Sophocles, Oedipus Rex
sources.description: A classical Greek tragedy that features the most famous encounter with the Sphinx.
sources.title: Sophocles, Oedipus Rex

sources.name: Apollodorus, Bibliotheca
sources.description: A comprehensive handbook of Greek mythology, providing a detailed account of the Oedipus myth.
sources.title: Apollodorus, Bibliotheca

sources.name: Hyginus, Fabulae
sources.description: A collection of mythological stories, offering various perspectives on the Sphinx's origins and role.
sources.title: Hyginus, Fabulae

sources.name: Pausanias, Description of Greece
sources.description: A travelogue and historical account of ancient Greece, offering insights into local beliefs and traditions, sometimes depicting alternative roles of the Sphinx.
sources.title: Pausanias, Description of Greece

sources.name: Hesiod, Theogony
sources.description: An early Greek poem that details the origins of the gods and mythical creatures, potentially providing background context.
sources.title: Hesiod, Theogony

sources.name: Fontenrose, Joseph. *Python: A Study of Delphic Myth and Its Origins*.
sources.description: A scholarly work analyzing the connections between riddles, prophecy, and monstrous figures like the Sphinx.
sources.title: Fontenrose, Joseph. *Python: A Study of Delphic Myth and Its Origins*.
